name: Urolithin A reduced runner recovery markers but did not significantly improve race performance
summary: A young-athlete urolithin A RCT adds useful limiting context: recovery and mitochondrial-pathway signals appeared, but the primary 3000 m performance endpoint was not significantly improved.
statement: In 42 highly trained male runners, 4 weeks of 1000 mg/day urolithin A did not significantly improve 3000 m time-trial performance, while reported secondary or exploratory signals included lower perceived exertion, reduced creatine kinase area under the curve, mitochondrial-pathway proteomic movement, and no change in measured mitochondrial function.
population_or_model: Highly trained male distance runners aged 18 to 40 years
quantitative_note: The abstract reports no significant 3000 m improvement in either group, lower rating of perceived exertion with urolithin A (p = 0.02), lower creatine kinase total area under the curve (p < 0.0001), and a medium effect for mitophagy markers without mitochondrial-function change.
caveats: The population is young and highly trained, not older, frail, or healthy-aging focused.
The primary performance outcome did not significantly improve.
Recovery and pathway signals do not establish durable aging benefit or mitophagy-mediated clinical improvement.
